scripts.subtitle: Classic PAS opener with annual leak math
scripts.original_vo: Listen. If you're a business owner sourcing from China, your bank is taking 4% of your annual budget. Every year. Here's the math. You spend 500K a year on Chinese suppliers. Your bank charges a 4% exchange rate margin. That's 20,000 dollars. Gone. Not on a fee line. Not on a statement. Baked into the rate so you never see it. Now run it over three years. That's 60,000 dollars your bank kept that should have stayed in your business. Stop. You can't negotiate this with your supplier. The supplier never saw that money. Your bank took it before the wire even left. The fix is not a harder PI negotiation. The fix is switching the wire rail. WorldFirst runs at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same 500K. Same suppliers. The leak drops to 2,000 dollars a year. You just got 18K back. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Picture this. A business owner in Malaysia just paid their Chinese supplier. The factory got the goods ready. Everyone's happy. But here's the part nobody talks about. The bank quietly took 4% of that payment. Not as a fee. Not on the statement. They hid it inside the exchange rate. Let's walk through the numbers slowly. Say a business owner spends 500,000 dollars a year buying from China. The bank charges a 4% margin on the currency swap from ringgit or USD into Chinese yuan. That's 20,000 dollars gone in one year. The supplier never saw that money. The bank took it before the payment even crossed the border. Now keep going. Year two, the leak repeats. Year three, the leak repeats. After three years, 60,000 dollars stayed with the bank. That money should have gone back into the business. More stock. A new hire. Better packaging. Gone. Here's the thing. A business owner cannot fix this by pushing the supplier for a better price. The supplier is not the leak. The bank is the leak. The fix is switching where the payment travels. WorldFirst, the payment service WorldFirst owns, charges only 0.3 to 0.5% on the same 500K, paying the same suppliers. Suddenly the annual leak drops from 20,000 to about 2,000 dollars. That's 18,000 a year back in the business. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.original_vo: Watch this. Your sourcing budget shrinks 4% every year and you don't even feel it. 500K to Chinese suppliers? Your bank quietly kept 20,000 dollars. Year two, your business grows 10%. You spend 550K. The leak grows with you. That's 22,000 gone. Year three, 605K spent, 24,200 leaked. Add it up. 66,200 dollars in three years. Not a fee. Not a charge. Just the exchange rate margin baked into every wire. And here's what kills me. You can't fix this by negotiating with your supplier. They never touched that money. Your bank took it on the conversion. The only fix is the rail itself. WorldFirst runs 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. Same suppliers. Same wires. Same supplier-side amount. Your annual leak goes from 20K to under 3K. Stop. Search WorldFirst. Period.

No alert. It's invisible because it's not a fee. It's a margin tucked into the rate. Here's the part most business owners miss. The supplier never had the money. The factory got paid the agreed amount. The 4% disappeared during the currency swap, before the payment even left the country. The bank took it on the conversion. WorldFirst, the international payment service, is built differently. 0.3 to 0.5% on the same kind of payments. The rate the business owner sees is close to the rate banks use with each other. That's the difference.
scripts.diff_markup: Here's <del>the truth</del><ins>something</ins> nobody's telling business owners about international wires. Your bank's biggest revenue line on your account is not your wire fee. It's your exchange rate. They quote you USD-CNY at a rate 4% worse than the real market rate. On 500K of sourcing, that's 20K a year. Compound it. Three years, 66K. Five years, over 110K. <del>Silent</del><ins>Quiet</ins>. No statement entry. No annual summary. You'll never see it because it's not a fee, it's a margin. And the <del>worst part</del><ins>part most business owners miss</ins>. You can't fix this by negotiating with your supplier. They never had the money. Your bank took it on the conversion. WorldFirst <del>doesn't play this game</del><ins>is built differently</ins>. 0.3 to 0.5% on the same wires. The rate you see is close to the rate banks use with each other. That's the difference<del>. Period</del>.

scripts.subtitle: Real talk + frustration escalation
scripts.original_vo: Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You're working harder every year and somehow keeping less. You added two new SKUs. You doubled marketing. You found a better factory. And your gross margin still went down. You know what nobody told you? Your bank takes 4% of your sourcing budget every year and the bigger you grow, the bigger they take. 500K spend, 20K to the bank. Scale to 1M, 40K to the bank. You're literally subsidising their growth with yours. Meanwhile you're losing sleep over a 50 cent unit price. Stop. The leak is the rail, not the factory. WorldFirst sits at 0.3 to 0.5% on the same wires. The 4% stops bleeding. Your scale starts compounding for you instead of for the bank. Search WorldFirst. Now.

You found a better factory. And your gross margin still went down. You know what nobody told you? Your bank takes 4% of your sourcing budget every year and the bigger you grow, the bigger they take. 500K spend, 20K to the bank. Scale to 1M, 40K to the bank. You're effectively subsidising their growth with yours. Meanwhile you're losing sleep over a 50 cent unit price. The leak is the rail, not the factory. scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here's something for every business owner in Malaysia sourcing from China. They're working harder every year and somehow keeping less. Sound familiar? Picture this. The owner added two new product lines. They doubled the marketing budget. They even found a better factory in Guangzhou. And the gross margin still dropped. Here's what nobody told them. The bank takes 4% of the sourcing budget every year through the exchange rate margin, the hidden gap inside the swap from ringgit or USD into Chinese yuan. The bigger the business grows, the bigger the bank's cut. Walk through it. On 500,000 dollars of sourcing, the bank takes 20,000 dollars a year. Scale up to one million dollars in sourcing, the bank takes 40,000 dollars. Double the work, double their take. The business is effectively subsidising the bank's growth with its own. Meanwhile, the owner is losing sleep over a 50 cent difference on a unit price. The leak is not the factory. The leak is the payment route. WorldFirst, the international payment service, sits at 0.3 to 0.5% on the same kind of payments. The 4% stops bleeding. The scale starts compounding for the business, not for the bank. Search WorldFirst.

You doubled marketing. You found a better factory. And your gross margin still went down. You know what nobody told you? Your bank takes 4% of your sourcing budget every year and the bigger you grow, the bigger they take. 500K spend, 20K to the bank. Scale to 1M, 40K to the bank. You're <del>literally</del><ins>effectively</ins> subsidising their growth with yours. Meanwhile you're losing sleep over a 50 cent unit price. <del>Stop. </del>The leak is the rail, not the factory. WorldFirst sits at 0.3 to 0.5% on the same wires. The 4% stops bleeding. Your scale starts compounding for you instead of for the bank. Search WorldFirst<del>. Now</del>.

PH1.analysis: The 4% margin figure is positioned as universal when card findings flag it as 'high-end SEA bank' not typical. Most Malaysian importers will not recognise their own bank at 4%, weakening trust on the first line. The 20K and 60K numbers are arithmetically correct off the 500K base but inherit the 4% assumption. 'Supplier never saw that money' framing is technically true and defensible. WorldFirst 0.3 to 0.5% claim is consistent with card findings. 'Search WorldFirst' close is brand-safe.

skeptic_comments.text: 4% on my Maybank? Don't think so leh. Maybe 1 to 2% only.
500K a year on suppliers, I wish lah. Small SME got nowhere near that.
Where this 4% from? Source please. Not just say.
Sound like ad. Show real rate comparison la, screenshot kind.
Who is WorldFirst? Got BNM license or not?

PH2.analysis: Compound growth math (66.2K over three years on 10% annual budget growth) is the highest-risk claim. Multiple assumptions stacked: that bank margin stays at 4%, that budget grows exactly 10% a year, that the business owner doesn't renegotiate or switch banks across three years. The 10% growth assumption is also a happy-path scenario, not all SMBs grow 10% YoY. 'Your annual leak goes from 20K to under 3K' is internally consistent (4% to 0.5% on 550K avg). Stacked claims make this script most likely to trigger skeptic pushback on the math.

skeptic_comments.text: Three years compound assume my business grow 10% every year. Got that easy meh.
66K over 3 years? Where the 4% assumption come from. CIMB charge me less.
If I really grow 10% a year I won't care about 4% lah.
Same suppliers same wires sound too clean. CN bank also got their own cut sometimes.
Show me one screenshot of WorldFirst rate vs my bank's rate. Then I believe.

PH3.analysis: 'Took 20,000 dollars from you' is strong framing for what is technically a disclosed FX margin, but defensible as opinion. 'You tried negotiating MOQ. You tried squeezing the PI.' is relatable and grounded. The 'supplier never saw that 4%' reframe is the strongest part of the script and is technically accurate. Main red flag is again the universal 4% number and 'before the money left your country' which is a slight oversimplification because conversion timing varies by rail and bank. The reframe lands harder than the math.

skeptic_comments.text: Didn't even notice? Bro of course I noticed my supplier got less. Just thought it was fees.
4% is high-end lah. Average importer probably 1.5 to 2.5%.
Supplier never saw the money, ok that part actually clear my doubt. Fair point.
Banks don't itemize FX margin? They do in fine print. Just nobody reads.
Who is WorldFirst? First time hearing. Got Malaysia license?

PH4.analysis: Cleanest mechanism explanation in the Hormozi set. The 'negotiating with the wrong person' reframe is psychologically strong and matches a real importer frustration. The 2% PI vs 4% bank comparison is illustrative and lands well. The 17K of 20K stays in business math (4% to 0.3%) is internally consistent. Main risk remains the universal 4% figure presented without qualifier. 'Vanished into the conversion before the wire crossed the border' is slightly poetic but defensible. Three-year 66K compound assumes constant 10% growth which is the same risk as PH2.

skeptic_comments.text: Wrong person to negotiate with, ok this point actually hit. Never thought of it that way.
4% every wire? My bank not so bad. Where's the data from?
Compound assume 10% YoY growth. Lots of SMEs flat or down 2025.
17K of 20K stays in business. So WorldFirst still take 3K. Got monthly fee?
Who is WorldFirst? Need to check if regulated here first.

PH5.analysis: The three-things-happening structure is clean and educational. 'Looking in the wrong place to plug it' frames the misdiagnosis well. 'You can't get the past years back. You can stop year four right now' is a sharp close. The 4% rate-vs-mid-market description is technically accurate framing. Main red flag is the universal 4% and the assumption that 100% of the 20K leak gets eliminated, when WorldFirst still has a 0.3 to 0.5% margin. The math (20K to under 3K) is consistent but the close implies more recovery than is real.

skeptic_comments.text: Year four stop right now sound nice but you assume I switch all my wires. Got annual contract with my bank.
4% worse than live market rate? Where you get the live market rate from. Reuters?
Under 3K leak. Means WorldFirst still got margin. Just smaller. Fine but say it lah.
Three things every wire is a clean breakdown. I like this one.
Who is WorldFirst? Sound American. Got SEA support?

PG1.analysis: Same core math as Hormozi (4%, 20K, 500K) so inherits same defensibility profile. The 'wrong fight wrong opponent' reframe is the strongest beat and is psychologically true. Softer brand close ('worth a look') reduces ad feel. The 1% MOQ negotiation vs 4% bank margin comparison is illustrative. Main risk is still universal 4% figure. 'The 4% stays in your pocket' overstates because WorldFirst still has 0.3 to 0.5% margin so really it's 3.5% to 3.7% that stays in pocket.

skeptic_comments.text: Wrong fight wrong opponent. Ok that part true. Never thought to fight with my bank.
4% on every wire? Where the number from? My bank not so bad.
500K sourcing a year, that's big SME. Small importer like me, maybe 80K to 150K.
4% stays in my pocket? But WorldFirst also take some right. Not free.
Worth a look feel less pushy than the others. But still want to see real rate.

PG2.analysis: 'Most expensive vendor is the bank' is an opinion claim with real teeth, framed as observation not fact. Compound growth math (66K over three years) carries same risk as PH2. 'That's a hire. That's a warehouse rack. That's your margin compression' is strong emotional ladder but doesn't overreach. 'You can't see it. It's not on the statement.' is largely true. Cleaner ending without 'Period' or call to action could be confusing but is intentional Gary Vee style. Strong overall, mid risk on the compound math.

skeptic_comments.text: Most expensive vendor is the bank. Damn, that hit. But got proof?
10% growth assumption again. My business flat this year actually.
Where this 4% from? Different banks charge differently lah.
Hire, warehouse rack, ok inspiration but I want numbers comparison please.
No call to action at the end? Where I go to check WorldFirst?

PG3.analysis: The 'try negotiating with your bank instead of supplier' frame is the standout angle in the set. It connects to a real importer behaviour (over-investing time in supplier negotiation while ignoring bank margin) and is psychologically defensible. 'You're fighting the wrong fight' is opinion not claim. '250K threshold' adds specificity. Main red flag is again universal 4% and 'on the same wires to the same factories' which slightly overstates because rail differences exist. Internal logic holds.

skeptic_comments.text: Negotiating with my bank, never tried that. They got dedicated relationship manager actually.
250K threshold ok that's helpful framing. I'm above that.
Same wires same factories on WorldFirst? Sure or not. Got different rail right.
4% margin everywhere lah. CIMB charge me about 1.5%.
Highest ROI hour this month, bit hyperbolic but I get the point.

PG4.analysis: 'Bank's biggest revenue line on your account is your exchange rate' is bold and arguably true for SME accounts, defensible as opinion. The 5-year 110K compound is the highest-stakes claim in the script and assumes constant 4% margin plus constant 10% growth. 'They never had the money' supplier reframe is the strongest and most defensible part. 'Rate banks use with each other' is technically interbank rate which WorldFirst doesn't actually give, but 'close to' was added in iteration which softens it correctly.

skeptic_comments.text: 110K over 5 years assume too many things. Cooked numbers.
Biggest revenue line on my account is the rate. Damn never thought about that.
Close to the rate banks use with each other. Soft hedge. Means they still take a cut lah.
Supplier never had the money, technically true. Good reframe.
Who is WorldFirst? Need to research. SEA team?

PG5.analysis: Cleanest script in the set. The 'working harder every year and keeping less' opener taps a real SME pain that's hard to argue with. The 'bigger you grow, the bigger they take' scaling beat is mathematically correct and emotionally resonant. 'Subsidising their growth with yours' is sharp opinion. Soft on specific defensible numbers (no compound, no 3-year extrapolation, just one 500K scenario and one 1M scenario). 'Effectively' was good iteration softening 'literally'. Most defensible script in the angle.

skeptic_comments.text: Working harder every year keeping less. Yes lah. Story of my 2025.
Bigger you grow bigger they take. Ya this part makes sense.
4% on every wire still high-end. Smaller importers might be 1.5 to 2%.
Subsidising their growth with mine. Damn ok this hits.
Who is WorldFirst? Got Malaysia office? Need to verify.
